html:
Clear-Span Hangar Roofs and Oilfield Storage Buildings in Pyote

Pyote's commercial roof stock splits into two very different categories - the handful of surviving structures tied to the old Rattlesnake Bomber Base, and the newer metal buildings oilfield companies have put up along I-20 since. Each category needs its own approach before we ever talk membrane options.

What's Left From the Air Base, and What's New

Pyote Army Airfield trained bomber crews during the Second World War and later served as a storage and scrapping yard for retired B-29 bombers, including the Enola Gay before it went on to the Smithsonian. Some of the surviving hangar and support structures from that era have found second lives as oilfield storage and salvage buildings, and their roofs are unlike anything else in this part of Ward County - wide clear-span steel over frames never designed with a modern recover system in mind.

Everything built since is a more ordinary story: pre-engineered metal shop buildings for oilfield service companies, a Monahans-Wickett-Pyote ISD building serving the town's kids, and small support structures along the highway. We treat those two categories completely differently when we scope a roof here.

Roofing a Building That Predates Modern Metal Building Codes

A clear-span hangar roof from the 1940s wasn't built to any current wind-uplift or insulation standard, and decades of patch work usually mean multiple roof materials layered over each other by the time we get there. Before we talk membrane options on one of these structures, we spend real time figuring out what's actually up there and whether the original steel can even carry another layer.

On a building with that kind of history, the honest scope sometimes starts with identifying what we don't yet know and need to find out, rather than handing over a clean number right away, because guessing on a structure this old is how owners end up paying twice. A first visit to one of these buildings is often as much about probing decking and purlins in a few representative spots as it is about looking at the roof surface itself.

Wide-Open Ward County Wind

Pyote sits in open country between Monahans and Wink, and wind exposure here is about as unobstructed as it gets in this part of Texas. Large-span roofs are especially vulnerable to uplift at ridge lines and eave edges, and that risk climbs further on any roof section that's already lost some of its original fastening pattern to age.

Add hail passes that move through this stretch of I-20 most spring seasons, and the older hangar-style roofs need a different inspection routine than a five-year-old metal shop building would. The structures we service most in Pyote fall into a short list:

  • Surviving hangar and support structures from the former air base, now used for oilfield storage
  • Newer pre-engineered metal shop buildings for oilfield service companies
  • A Monahans-Wickett-Pyote ISD building
  • Small highway-frontage support buildings along I-20
  • Salvage and laydown structures tied to older base-era buildings

Guessing on an Eighty-Year-Old Roof Gets Expensive Fast

Misjudging a historic hangar roof's remaining capacity, or underbidding a recover on one of these structures, tends to surface later as a water or structural problem that costs far more than a careful inspection up front would have. We'd rather document exactly what a roof will and won't support before we recommend anything, especially tear-off and replacement work on a structure this old.

For newer shop buildings, the calculus is simpler and more like anywhere else in the Odessa market - the risk is mostly about catching wind and hail damage before it becomes a full tear-off. We treat that decision with the same directness whether the building is five years old or eighty.

Owners who bought or leased one of the old base-era structures without a full understanding of what's holding the roof up sometimes find that out the hard way, mid-project, when a section of decking turns out softer than expected. We'd rather find that during a walk-through with a ladder and a flashlight than during a tear-off with a crew already mobilized and a schedule already committed.

Questions From Pyote Building Owners

Can you actually roof a structure that old without full engineering drawings?

We can inspect, document what's visibly there, and give an honest assessment of what we can and can't verify. On structures without original drawings, we stay conservative and recommend an engineer's review before any major structural change to the roof system.

Is it worth saving one of the old hangar-style buildings, or better to replace it?

That depends on the steel condition underneath, which we can only judge after a real inspection. Some of these structures still have sound frames worth re-roofing; others have corrosion or damage that makes a new pre-engineered building the more practical long-term answer.

How is roofing a newer Pyote shop building different from the historic structures?

It's simpler and faster to scope - modern materials, known specs, straightforward wind and hail exposure. The historic buildings require more investigation before we can even talk options.

What's the biggest weather risk for a Pyote roof?

Wind uplift on large open spans, especially at ridges and eaves, followed by spring hail. The open terrain here gives wind very little to break against before it hits a roof.

Do you handle both small oilfield shops and larger legacy structures?

Yes, though the process looks different for each - a small modern shop gets a standard inspection and clear options, while a legacy structure gets a more careful, sometimes multi-visit assessment before we recommend anything.
